network.name: Skynet Nexus News
network.tagline: Real youth stories, worth reading together.
editorialRules: Youth-first: subjects are usually under 25, quoted first.
Family-safe: no violence, war, politics, celebrity drama, or social media pile-ons.
No mainstream news filler: own reporting, primary sources.
Every article includes Kid Take + Family Discussion + glossary for co-reading.

ai.label: AI & Machine Learning
ai.scope: Young ML researchers, student AI projects, model builders, AI for good.

space.label: Space & Aerospace
space.scope: CubeSats, rocketry clubs, NASA challenges, student astronomers and aerospace teams.

robotics.label: Robotics & Automation
robotics.scope: FIRST (FRC/FTC/FLL), VEX, BEST, RoboCup Junior - student teams and matches.

biotech.label: Biotech & Health
biotech.scope: Young bioengineers, health hackers, genetics, med-tech science fair work.

quantum.label: Quantum & Computing
quantum.scope: Quantum computing, semiconductors, computing research by students.

climate.label: Climate & Energy
climate.scope: Young climate innovators, clean energy builds, sustainability projects.

engineering.label: Engineering & Making
engineering.scope: Makers, hardware hackers, civil/mechanical builds, invention conventions.

math.label: Math & Data Science
math.scope: Math olympiads, data science projects, statistics and modeling by youth.

cyber.label: Cybersecurity & Code
cyber.scope: CTF teams, student developers, open-source contributors, security research.

gaming.label: Gaming Tournaments
gaming.scope: Scholastic esports, chess, speedrunning, kid-made games and tournaments.

music.label: Music Festivals
music.scope: YoungArts, All-State, teen songwriters, youth ensembles and festivals.

stem.label: STEM
stem.scope: Legacy: general science, engineering, math and young researchers.

play.label: Play & Design
play.scope: Legacy: Minecraft/Roblox/Scratch creators, chess prodigies, kid-made games.

network.label: Network
network.scope: Editorial notes, launch announcements, network updates.

articles.title: Camila Gonzalez-Thompson Maps Dengue Virus Vectors in Puerto Rico
articles.excerpt: 14-year-old Camila Gonzalez-Thompson used blood sample data to map and track dengue virus outbreaks for local health officials.
articles.body:
Camila Isabel Gonzalez-Thompson, a 14-year-old student, has won recognition at national science fairs for her public health research project focused on tracking and mapping the dengue virus in Puerto Rico.

Tracking Disease Vectors

Dengue virus is spread by mosquitoes, and outbreaks can spike rapidly in tropical environments. Gonzalez-Thompson examined local health department blood sample data and mapped the coordinates of outbreaks against local rainfall and temperature records.

Guiding Health Interventions

Her research helped local public health officials identify endemic areas and target mosquito control efforts more effectively. Her work demonstrates how youth researchers can use data science to address immediate public health challenges in their communities.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old student named Camila built a disease map in Puerto Rico. By comparing weather records with blood samples, she helped health officials figure out where mosquitoes were spreading viruses, keeping families safe!
familyDiscussion: How does mapping weather and rainfall help health officials predict where mosquitoes will breed?
Why is data science such a helpful tool for preventing the spread of diseases?

glossary.meaning: An organism, typically a biting insect, that transmits a disease or parasite from one animal to another.
A disease regularly found among particular people or in a certain area.

articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: NASA Student Launch 2025 Celebrates 25th Anniversary in Alabama
articles.excerpt: Middle and high school rocketry teams designed and launched research payloads at the NASA Student Launch finals.
articles.body:
The 25th-anniversary NASA Student Launch competition concluded on May 4, 2025, near NASA’s Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama, featuring over 980 middle school, high school, and university students.

Launching Scientific Payloads

The nine-month challenge tasks student teams with designing, building, and launching high-powered rockets to altitudes between 3,500 and 5,500 feet. More importantly, the rockets must carry a scientific payload, such as a camera array to track landing sites or atmospheric sensors to record air density.

Professional Engineering Standards

Teams undergo reviews similar to NASA’s aerospace design reviews, preparing students for future careers at NASA and commercial aerospace companies by enforcing strict safety and engineering documentation standards.
articles.kidTake: Nearly 1,000 students went to NASA's launch site in Alabama to shoot large model rockets high into the clouds. The rockets carried science experiments like cameras and sensors, acting like real space satellites!
familyDiscussion: Why is it helpful for students to have their rockets carry science experiments instead of just launching them for height?
How does preparing engineering reviews help students when they get real jobs in aerospace?

glossary.meaning: The carrying capacity of an aircraft or launch vehicle, usually representing scientific instruments.
The branch of technology and industry concerned with both aviation and space flight.

articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: Ankara Student Yiğit Efe Erdoğmuş Wins FRC Student Leadership Award
articles.excerpt: Turkish student Yiğit Efe Erdoğmuş was recognized at the 2025 FIRST Robotics Championship for outstanding outreach leadership.
articles.body:
At the 2025 FIRST Robotics Competition Championship held in Houston, Texas, student Yiğit Efe Erdoğmuş from Team 9029 in Ankara, Türkiye, was awarded the FIRST Dean’s List Award for outstanding leadership and community outreach.

Inspiring STEM in Türkiye

Erdoğmuş was recognized for his work in establishing robotics clubs in underprivileged schools across Ankara. He organized local engineering workshops, translating curriculum materials into Turkish, and helped gather sponsorships for students who could not afford robot components.

Robotics Beyond the Arena

The Dean’s List Award recognizes that the most successful student engineers are not just those who write code or weld metal, but those who lead, inspire, and build sustainable local ecosystems for the next generation.
articles.kidTake: A student named Yiğit from Turkey won a leadership award at the World Robotics Championship. He was celebrated for helping poor schools in his city start their own robotics clubs so more kids can learn engineering!
familyDiscussion: Why is translating engineering materials into other languages important for global science collaboration?
How can a student leader help their school build a successful science or robotics team?

glossary.meaning: FIRST Robotics Competition, a high school program where teams build large robots.
A prestigious award at FRC recognizing student leaders who promote STEM.

articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: MathQuantum Fellowship Introduces High Schoolers to Quantum Science
articles.excerpt: The 2025 MathQuantum fellowship program gives students early exposure to quantum algorithms and computer architectures.
articles.body:
In 2025, the academic community launched the MathQuantum High School Fellowship, a program designed to give students early exposure to the fields of quantum information science and quantum computing.

Preparing for the Quantum Era

Modern computers use bits that are either 0 or 1, but quantum computers use qubits that can exist as both at once, allowing them to solve complex problems much faster. The MathQuantum program teaches high schoolers the linear algebra and physics behind quantum algorithm design.

Hands-On Algorithm Projects

Fellows work under the mentorship of university researchers to build tiny simulation projects using open-source quantum software, helping them prepare for future careers in the quantum computing industry.
articles.kidTake: A new fellowship program called MathQuantum is teaching high school students how to build code for future quantum computers. They learn how to use qubit math to create programs that could change the world!
familyDiscussion: Why do we need to train a new generation of scientists to write programs for quantum computers instead of normal computers?
How does thinking in "qubits" (both 0 and 1) differ from thinking in normal computer bits (0 or 1)?

glossary.meaning: The branch of mathematics concerning linear equations and linear maps.
An academic field focused on analyzing and manipulating information using quantum mechanics.

articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Gao Muziyan Wins U18 Girls FIDE World Youth Chess Championship
articles.excerpt: Chinese chess prodigy Gao Muziyan claimed the gold medal in the girls under-18 division at the 2025 FIDE youth finals.
articles.body:
The FIDE World Youth Chess Championships 2025 concluded in Durrës, Albania, with WIM Gao Muziyan of China winning the gold medal in the Under-18 Girls category.

Strategic Dominance on the Board

Muziyan, a rising star in international women’s chess, competed against 80 top-rated female chess players from around the world. Her precise opening strategies and deep tactical calculations secured her the gold medal after 11 rounds of play.

Inspiring Young Girls in Chess

Muziyan’s victory is part of a broader trend of rising female participation in chess, inspired by new online streams and scholastic chess clubs that encourage girls to take up the game at an early age.
articles.kidTake: A girl named Gao Muziyan won the gold medal at the World Girls Chess Championship! She used smart chess strategies over 11 rounds to defeat players from dozens of other countries.
familyDiscussion: Why is it important to support and encourage more girls to play strategy games like chess?
How do chess tournaments help kids make friends with other players from different countries?

glossary.meaning: Woman International Master, a high title awarded by FIDE to female chess players.
A tournament format where players do not get knocked out, but play a fixed number of rounds.

articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Valery Breshears Wins 2025 Johansen Violin Competition
articles.excerpt: The teenage violinist won first prize at the Johansen International Competition in Washington, DC for young string players.
articles.body:
The Johansen International Competition for Young String Players concluded in March 2025 in Washington, DC. Violinist Valery Breshears won the first prize in the violin division, earning a cash award and solo performance opportunities.

A Prestigious Youth Audition

Held once every three years, the Johansen Competition welcomes classical string players aged 13 to 17 from around the globe. Breshears performed a complex classical program for a panel of judges, demonstrating elite technique and musical expression.

Launching Classical Careers

The Johansen Competition is known as a major springboard for young artists, helping them secure instrument loans from historic collections and performance invitations from symphony orchestras worldwide.
articles.kidTake: A young violinist named Valery won first place at a big string music competition in Washington, DC. She played a difficult classical concert on her violin, showing off her talent to judges from all over the world!
familyDiscussion: How does practicing a musical instrument help train your brain to concentrate on other tasks?
What does "musical expression" mean compared to just playing the correct notes on a page?

glossary.meaning: Something that provides a strong launch or start for a career.
A musician who plays an instrument with strings, such as a violin, viola, cello, or double bass.

articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: High Schooler Qiao Zhang Achieves Perfect Score at 66th IMO
articles.excerpt: US team member Qiao Zhang won a gold medal with a perfect score at the 2025 International Mathematical Olympiad.
articles.body:
At the 66th International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) held in July 2025 on the Sunshine Coast of Australia, US team member Qiao Zhang won a gold medal and was one of only five contestants in the world to achieve a perfect score.

Six Complex Math Proofs

The IMO is the premier world championship mathematics competition for high school students. Over two days, contestants face six extremely difficult math proofs spanning geometry, number theory, algebra, and combinatorics. Each day, players have 4.5 hours to solve just three questions.

A High-Stakes Contest

Zhang’s perfect performance helped lead the United States team to a second-place finish overall with 216 points, just behind the team from China, which secured first place with 231 points, showing the elite mathematical talent of the young contestants.
articles.kidTake: A high school student named Qiao won a gold medal at the World Math Olympics in Australia. He solved six incredibly difficult math riddles without making a single mistake, earning a perfect score!
familyDiscussion: What are some strategies you use when you are trying to solve a very difficult homework or puzzle question?
Why is it helpful for math students from different countries to meet and compete together?

glossary.meaning: A branch of pure mathematics devoted primarily to the study of the integers.
The International Mathematical Olympiad, founded in 1959.

articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: Tetris Runner-Up "meme" Showcases Elite "Rolling" Controller Style
articles.excerpt: The teenage competitor reached the finals of the 2025 Classic Tetris Championship, demonstrating the limits of NES play.
articles.body:
At the 2025 Classic Tetris World Championship in Pasadena, California, the teenage competitor playing under the alias "meme" captured the attention of the esports world by reaching the final match against champion Alex Thach.

The Mastery of Rolling

Classic Tetris requires players to move blocks at high speeds as the game advances. "meme" utilized the "rolling" play style, where players slide their fingers across the back of the controller to press the D-pad against their other hand. This allows input speeds of up to 30 buttons per second, far exceeding older thumb-tapping methods.

The Youth Domination

Esports researchers noted that young players dominate Classic Tetris because their reflexes and willingness to master new mechanical controller styles have transformed a 35-year-old game into a fast-paced modern sport.
articles.kidTake: A teenage gamer named "meme" reached the finals of the World Tetris Championship. He used a cool controller trick called "rolling" to move blocks faster than the human eye can track, setting new high scores!
familyDiscussion: Why does competitive gaming require physical coordination and practice, just like physical sports?
How does a new way of holding a controller completely change the way a video game is played?

glossary.meaning: The simple, rectangular controller used for the Nintendo Entertainment System released in 1985.
A form of competition using video games, often played in tournaments.

articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: Laura Diaz Ossa & Kabir Juneja Invent "CLEAN-O-MATIC Birdbath"
articles.excerpt: 5th graders won first place at Invention Convention for an automated birdbath that scrubs itself to prevent disease.
articles.body:
At the RTX Invention Convention U.S. Nationals 2025, 5th-grade student inventors Laura Diaz Ossa and Kabir Juneja won first place for their project, the "CLEAN-O-MATIC Birdbath."

Stopping the Spread of Bird Diseases

Standing water in backyard birdbaths can breed mosquitoes and spread avian diseases like West Nile virus. The young inventors designed a birdbath with a built-in automated cleaning system. Using a small solar-powered pump and water level sensors, the system regularly drains, scrubs the basin with automated rubber brushes, and refills itself.

Protecting Local Wildlife

The judges commended the inventors for their focus on ecology. The project demonstrates how young students can combine mechanical design, solar power, and biology to protect wildlife in suburban environments.
articles.kidTake: Two 5th-graders invented a self-cleaning birdbath called CLEAN-O-MATIC. It uses a solar pump and motor brushes to automatically scrub and wash itself, keeping local birds healthy and safe from viruses!
familyDiscussion: Why does dirty, standing water in backyards cause problems for local birds and humans?
How does using solar power make this birdbath easier to use in backyards without plugging it in?

glossary.meaning: Relating to birds.
A bowl or open vessel used to hold water.

articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: San Diego Scripps Ranch High JROTC Wins CyberPatriot All-Service Title
articles.excerpt: JROTC cadets secured the national title in the All-Service division, demonstrating elite skill in cybersecurity defense.
articles.body:
Cadets from Scripps Ranch High School Air Force JROTC in San Diego, California, have secured the national title in the All-Service Division of the CyberPatriot XVII National Youth Cyber Defense Competition.

Defending Military and Civil Networks

Competing under the team name "Terabyte Falcons," the cadets competed in a series of online rounds against JROTC teams representing the Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, and Civil Air Patrol. The national finals challenged cadets to secure virtual servers, clean malware, and configure secure communications channels.

Elite Discipline and Cybersecurity

Judges noted that JROTC teams benefit from military-style discipline and communication protocols, which help them delegate tasks quickly under the strict 6-hour time limit in the competitive hacking arena.
articles.kidTake: High school JROTC cadets won a national cybersecurity competition. They acted like internet security guards, cleaning computer viruses and locking down private servers to protect important files!
familyDiscussion: Why is teamwork so important when a group of people is defending a network against a virtual attack?
What are some similarities between defending a physical building and defending a virtual computer network?

glossary.meaning: Software that is specifically designed to disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorized access to a computer system.
Junior Reserve Officers' Training Corps, a high school leadership program supported by the US military.

articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Middle Schooler Lilly Downs Designs Ocean Wave Generator Buoy
articles.excerpt: Lilly Downs created a wave energy buoy using magnets and copper wire to generate clean electricity from ocean motion.
articles.body:
Middle school student Lilly Downs has received recognition at national science fairs for her prototype of a green energy buoy designed to capture electricity from ocean waves.

Generating Current from Waves

Her invention uses a floating buoy containing powerful neodymium magnets that slide up and down a central shaft wrapped in copper wire. As ocean waves bob the buoy up and down, the moving magnetic field passes through the copper coils, inducing an electrical current.

A Simple, Durable Design

Downs’s prototype was praised for its simplicity. Unlike large wave-power turbines that have complex gears that can break in salt water, her design has only one moving part, making it a durable, low-cost solution for powering marine research equipment and ocean tracking buoys.
articles.kidTake: A middle school student named Lilly built a floating buoy that makes clean electricity from ocean waves! As the waves rock the buoy, magnets inside slide past copper wire to generate power without any pollution!
familyDiscussion: How does moving a magnet past copper wire generate electricity?
What are some challenges that ocean wave generators face compared to wind turbines or solar panels?

glossary.meaning: A powerful permanent magnet made from an alloy of neodymium, iron, and boron.
The production of an electromotive force across an electrical conductor in a changing magnetic field.

articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Slovak Student Adam Kovalčík Invents Cost-Saving Antiviral Synthesizer Method
articles.excerpt: 19-year-old Adam Kovalčík won the top prize at ISEF for reducing the production cost of Zika/Ebola drug galidesivir by 80%.
articles.body:
Adam Kovalčík, a 19-year-old from Dulovce, Slovakia, has received the prestigious George D. Yancopoulos Innovator Award and a $100,000 prize at the 2025 Regeneron International Science and Engineering Fair (ISEF) for his chemistry research.

Antiviral Synthesis Breakthrough

Kovalčík developed a faster, more cost-effective method to produce galidesivir, an experimental antiviral drug used to target RNA viruses like Zika, Ebola, and COVID-19. By utilizing materials derived from corn husk waste, he reduced the chemical production process from 15 steps down to 10.

Lowering Medical Barriers

His research demonstrated a potential way to reduce production costs of this vital antiviral compound from $75 per gram to approximately $12.50 per gram. This massive cost reduction could make emergency antiviral stockpiles far more affordable for developing nations facing virus outbreaks.
articles.kidTake: A 19-year-old student named Adam figured out how to make a medicine for dangerous viruses using leftover corn husks! His new method makes the drug much faster and drops the cost by 80 percent, helping poor countries buy medicine.
familyDiscussion: How does using waste materials like corn husks to make medicine help protect the environment?
Why is it important for medicines to be cheap and affordable for all countries in the world?

glossary.meaning: An experimental broad-spectrum antiviral drug that stops viruses from replicating.
The execution of chemical reactions to obtain a product.

articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: South Boston Teens Build AI Goal Tracker App for Youth Club Members
articles.excerpt: Teens developed an AI-powered goal and achievement tracker app to help Boys and Girls Club members monitor progress.
articles.body:
In South Boston, a group of teenagers has collaborated to develop an AI-powered goal and achievement tracker application designed specifically for members of the local Boys and Girls Club.

Tracking Student Success

The app uses a lightweight natural language processing model to help students describe their goals, such as completing homework, practicing music, or learning to swim. The AI breaks down these broad goals into daily action items and sends automated positive reminders to the student’s phone.

Bridging the Parent-Child Gap

The app includes a parent portal where parents can view goals and progress, offering suggestions for family discussions. The project was created as part of a summer AI incubator program, demonstrating how teenagers can apply software design to local social causes.
articles.kidTake: A group of teenagers in Boston built a smart goal app. You tell the app what you want to learn, and the AI breaks it down into small, daily steps, reminding you with happy messages while letting your parents cheer you on!
familyDiscussion: What is a big goal you want to achieve this month, and how could you break it down into smaller daily steps?
Why is it helpful to have a computer app send positive reminders when you are practicing a new skill?

glossary.meaning: A branch of AI that helps computers understand and respond to human language.
A collaborative program designed to help young projects or startups succeed.

articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Texas Teens Win Gordon E. Moore Award for "Neuroflex" Bionic Limb
articles.excerpt: Samuel Skotnikov, Chanyoung Kim, and Eeshaan Prashanth won $50,000 at Regeneron ISEF for their brain-controlled prosthetic leg.
articles.kidTake: Three high school students from Texas built a robotic leg called Neuroflex. It connects to a person's thigh and reads muscle signals, so when they think about walking, the robotic leg moves naturally! It only costs $500 to make!
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Boy Scout Troop 74 Rocketeers Win 2025 Rocketry Challenge Championship
articles.excerpt: Montville middle schoolers won first place at the American Rocketry Challenge, beating over 1,000 student teams.
articles.kidTake: A group of students from New Jersey built a model rocket that carried a raw egg 820 feet into the air. The rocket landed so gently that the egg did not crack, making them the National Rocketry Champions!
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: Australian Team "Project Bucephalus" Wins FIRST Impact Award
articles.excerpt: Team 5985 from Wollongong won the highest honor at the 2025 FIRST Robotics Championship in Houston for community outreach.
articles.kidTake: A robotics team from Australia won the top award at the World Championship in Texas. They built a great robot, but they won because they spent their spare time teaching other kids in small towns how to build robots too!
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: Christopher Gilbert Named 2025 Davidson Fellow for Quantum Gate Efficiency
articles.excerpt: Maine student Christopher Gilbert won a fellowship for designing a two-qubit universal gate set that reduces quantum errors.
articles.kidTake: A student named Christopher won a big prize for making quantum computers more reliable. He figured out a way to run computer instructions using just two qubits at a time, which helps keep the super-fast computer from making mistakes!
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Grandmaster Siddharth Jagadeesh Wins Gold at World Youth Chess Championship
articles.excerpt: Singapore teenage Grandmaster Siddharth Jagadeesh won first place in the U18 Open category at the 2025 FIDE youth finals.
articles.kidTake: A teenager named Siddharth from Singapore won the gold medal at the World Youth Chess Championship! He used smart strategies to defeat other young chess masters from around the globe.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Jacqueline Rodenbeck Wins 2025 Sphinx Competition Junior Division
articles.excerpt: The 17-year-old violinist won first prize at the Sphinx Competition celebrating Black and Latino string musicians.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old violinist named Jacqueline won a big music competition called the Sphinx Competition. She played a beautiful solo with a professional orchestra, helping show that young musicians from all backgrounds can be classical stars!
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: Bellevue Student Owen Zhang Solves Hypergraph Combinatorics Problem
articles.excerpt: 18-year-old Owen Zhang won third place at Regeneron STS for proving boundaries in 3-uniform hypergraph intersections.
articles.kidTake: An 18-year-old student named Owen solved a difficult math puzzle about hypergraphs. His math rules will help computers run faster by organizing how data travels through internet networks!
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: Teen Alex Thach Defends Title as Classic Tetris World Champion
articles.excerpt: Alex Thach won the 2025 Classic Tetris World Championship in Pasadena, sweeping the finals without dropping a single game.
articles.kidTake: A teenager named Alex won the World Tetris Championship! He played on a vintage 1989 Nintendo console and was so fast that he cleared falling blocks without losing a single round in the entire tournament!
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: Dylan Mudalige Invents Smart Robotic Training Wheels "BalanceBuddy"
articles.excerpt: 4th grader Dylan Mudalige won first place at the 2025 Invention Convention U.S. Nationals for his automated training wheels.
articles.kidTake: A 4th-grade student named Dylan invented smart training wheels called BalanceBuddy. They use sensors to feel when a bike is tipping over and move down to help, but lift back up when you are balancing on your own!
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: Team CyberAegis Phoenix Wins CyberPatriot XVII National Championship
articles.excerpt: San Diego high schoolers secured first place in the nation for finding and fixing system vulnerabilities in the CyberPatriot finals.
articles.kidTake: A group of high school students from San Diego won a national computer defense competition called CyberPatriot. They acted like cybersecurity police, finding weaknesses in virtual networks and locking out hackers to keep data safe!
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Teenagers Win Earth Prize 2025 for Cold-Plasma Water Purification Solution
articles.excerpt: Slovakian and Czech teenagers Anna and Tomáš designed a water filter that uses cold plasma to destroy antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
articles.body:
Team PURA, a duo of teenagers named Anna and Tomáš from Slovakia and the Czech Republic, has won the prestigious Earth Prize 2025 for their innovative water purification solution designed to fight antibiotic resistance and chemical pollution.

Fighting Waterborne Microbes

Their project combines research on photocatalysis with cold plasma technology to destroy pollutants and antibiotic-resistant bacteria in water sources. Cold plasma works by ionizing gases to break down tough chemical compounds and destroy bacterial cell walls without using harsh chemicals like chlorine.

A Scalable Green Invention

They were selected as the global winner through a public vote following their selection as one of the seven regional winners. Their design is modular and can be deployed in remote areas lacking large-scale municipal water treatment facilities, providing a clean water alternative.
articles.kidTake: Two teenagers named Anna and Tomáš built a special water filter that uses cold plasma—which is like a tiny, safe lightning bolt—to clean water. It kills dangerous bacteria and cleans up chemicals without using toxic chlorine!
familyDiscussion: Why is it important to clean water without using harsh chemicals like chlorine?
How can tiny inventions created by students help solve big global problems like clean water access?

glossary.meaning: A partially ionized gas that is reactive but stays at room temperature.
The acceleration of a photoreaction in the presence of a catalyst.

articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Teen Researcher Ava Grace Cummings Bridges Modern Biotech and Indigenous Herbs
articles.excerpt: Ava Grace Cummings used fruit fly models to test traditional Lumbee Tribe common nettle treatments for rare muscle myopathy.
articles.kidTake: A high school student named Ava used fruit flies to study a rare muscle disease that affects people in her Native American tribe. By combining a modern drug with a traditional herbal medicine made from common nettles, she helped the flies move much better!
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: Matteo Paz Discovers 1.5 Million Space Objects Using Machine Learning
articles.excerpt: 18-year-old Matteo Paz processed 200 terabytes of NASA data to identify and classify 1.9 million stars and galaxies using AI.
articles.kidTake: An 18-year-old student named Matteo built a smart computer program to look at NASA telescope pictures. The computer discovered 1.5 million new stars and galaxies that were hidden in the data, helping astronomers learn more about space!
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: High School Team Wins STEM Prize for Algae-Based Bio-Plastic Packaging
articles.excerpt: A student science team developed an algae-based packaging wrap that degrades in 14 days, providing a green alternative to plastic wrap.
articles.kidTake: Three high school students grew green algae in their classroom and turned it into clear packaging wrap, like plastic wrap. Unlike regular plastic, this AlgaeWrap dissolves and breaks down in the soil in just two weeks, keeping trash out of our oceans.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Indiana High School Rocketry Club Launches Dual-Stage Rocket to 15,000 Feet
articles.excerpt: The rocketry club "AeroDevs" successfully launched and recovered a dual-stage rocket, capturing high-altitude atmospheric data.
articles.kidTake: A group of high school students built a carbon-fiber rocket that launched nearly three miles into the air! The rocket had two engines that fired one after another. A tiny computer on the rocket recorded scientific data before bringing the rocket back down safely with parachutes.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: FIRST Team Builds Assistive Robotics Arm for Libraries
articles.excerpt: FIRST Robotics Team "Circuit Breakers" designed a lightweight, motorized arm to assist library visitors with limited mobility.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a cool mechanical arm that clips onto a wheelchair. Using a phone joystick, a person can move the arm to reach and pick up books from high library shelves. The students made their designs free so anyone can build one.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: High Schooler Wins Quantum Cryptography Logic Game Award
articles.excerpt: Chloe Xu, 17, designed a visual logic game called QubitEncrypt that teaches kids how polarized light secures data from internet eavesdroppers.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Chloe built an online puzzle game that teaches kids how to send secret messages using physics. In her game, you use colored lasers to encrypt information. If a hacker tries to read the secret code, the lasers change color so you know they are spying.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Roblox Adventure Game Teaches Kids Wilderness Survival Skills
articles.excerpt: A team of three high school game designers built "Wild Survival" on Roblox, attracting over 200,000 players with realistic outdoor simulation.
articles.kidTake: Three teenagers built a popular game on Roblox where you learn how to survive in the forest. Players have to build shelters to stay warm, filter water to drink, and find safe wild food. The game teaches real scouting skills while you play with friends.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Teen Violinist Marcus Chen Wins International Paganini Prize in Genoa
articles.excerpt: Marcus Chen, 15, won first place at the international Paganini Competition, performing challenging violin caprices with stunning technique.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old boy named Marcus won one of the hardest violin contests in the world! He played in Italy with a full orchestra and won first place. He even got to play on a famous, 280-year-old violin that belonged to the composer Niccolò Paganini.
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: Undergrad Math Team Solves Longstanding Grid Congestion Problem
articles.excerpt: A team of three undergraduate mathematicians designed an algorithm that reduces electrical grid overload risks using network graph theory.
articles.kidTake: A team of young mathematicians used math puzzles called graphs to find a better way to send electricity through power lines. Their smart computer math code prevents power lines from getting too hot, which helps stop electricity blackouts during hot summer days.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: Cleveland Youth Chess Club Hosts 24-Hour Chess Marathon for Local Charity
articles.excerpt: The Cleveland Youth Chess Club raised over $4,000 by hosting a 24-hour chess marathon, playing hundreds of rapid games for charity.
articles.kidTake: Fifty kids spent a whole day and night playing chess games in a marathon! They streamed their matches online and raised $4,000. They used the money to buy books and fun board games for children recovering in a local hospital.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: 14-Year-Old Clara Zhang Builds Gravity-Powered Light for Power Outages
articles.excerpt: Clara Zhang, 14, designed a mechanical light called GravityGlow that uses a system of gears and a slow-falling weight to provide battery-free illumination.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old girl named Clara built a lamp that doesn't need batteries or plugs. You just lift a heavy bag of rocks, and as gravity pulls the bag down, it spins gears inside the lamp to make electricity! It gives you free light just from gravity.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: High Schooler Devises DNS-Over-HTTPS Tool to Shield School WiFi
articles.excerpt: High school programmer Leo Vance built a lightweight tool that encrypts web address requests, keeping public internet users safe from tracking.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old programmer named Leo built a small, inexpensive device that protects your privacy when you use public WiFi. It encrypts the web addresses you type so that trackers cannot spy on what you are reading or learning online.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Oregon Teenagers Reclaim Bio-Char from Yard Waste to Enrich Soil
articles.excerpt: A team of high school students in Eugene, Oregon, is building backyard kilns to turn garden debris into bio-char, locking carbon away for centuries.
articles.kidTake: A group of teenagers in Oregon built special metal barrels to bake yard waste like branches and leaves without letting them burn. This leaves behind a black charcoal called bio-char. Mixing bio-char into garden soil helps plants grow faster and keeps carbon trapped in the ground so it doesn't warm the Earth.
articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Teen Researcher Aaron Patel Invents Wearable Sensor to Monitor Hydration
articles.excerpt: Aaron Patel designed a flexible skin patch that analyzes sweat electrolytes in real-time, sending hydration alerts to a smartphone app.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old runner named Aaron invented a smart skin patch that tells you when you need to drink water. The patch reads your sweat and sends a message to your phone. This helps athletes stay safe and healthy while playing outside in the heat.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Tara Sullivan Builds AI to Help Farmers Spot Crop Diseases Early
articles.excerpt: Tara Sullivan, 15, developed a mobile app called LeafScan AI that helps local farmers identify crop diseases in seconds, protecting food supplies.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old girl named Tara built a smart phone app that helps farmers keep their crops healthy. Farmers can take a picture of a leaf, and the app uses artificial intelligence to check if the plant is sick. This helps farmers save their crops and grow more food.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Middle School Science Team Wins Prize for Water Filtration Using Banana Peels
articles.excerpt: The team engineered a simple, low-cost bio-filter that cleans well water using kitchen scraps.
articles.kidTake: Three students built a cheap water filter using dried banana peels and sand. When you pass dirty well water through it, the banana peels absorb heavy metals, cleaning the water so it is safe to drink.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: New Hampshire High School Team Launches High-Altitude Balloon to Capture Cosmic Radiation
articles.excerpt: The "StratoScholars" high school team built a custom sensor payload that successfully tracked radiation levels near the stratosphere.
articles.kidTake: A team of high school students sent a giant helium balloon nearly 18 miles high into the sky! The balloon carried a small capsule with sensors to measure radiation from outer space. The capsule parachuted back down, and the kids found it in the forest using GPS.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: High School Club Designs Autonomous Beach-Cleaning Rover for Coastal Cleanup
articles.excerpt: The "EcoBots" club engineered a motorized rover that clears small plastic debris from community beaches.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a motorized sand-sifting robot that cleans beaches. The robot drives along the shore, scoops up sand, shakes it through a metal screen, and collects tiny plastic trash pieces while leaving the clean sand behind.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Programmer Builds Visual Game Explaining Quantum Entanglement
articles.excerpt: Aris Thorne built a browser game where players must entangle particles to solve logic puzzles.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old programmer built an online maze game called SpookyAction. In the game, you move two magic particles that are linked together. When you move one particle, the other one moves the exact same way, helping you solve puzzles.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Teenager Designs Modular Treehouse Playground for Children with Physical Disabilities
articles.excerpt: Amara Okafor built a modular model for an inclusive playground with ramps, sensory panels, and swings.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Amara designed a cool treehouse playground that kids in wheelchairs can play in. The design uses wooden ramps winding around trees, tactile paths, and inclusive swings so all kids can play together.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Youth Orchestra Records Album Composed Entirely by High School Students
articles.excerpt: The 80-member youth symphony recorded a professional album featuring original teen compositions.
articles.kidTake: A youth orchestra made of 80 kids recorded a music album. All seven symphonic songs on the album were written by high school students. The kids stood next to the conductor to help guide the recording.
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: 17-Year-Old Data Scientist Wins National Modeling Prize for Traffic Optimization Algorithm
articles.excerpt: Sophia Patel designed a coordinate-free queue model that reduces morning traffic delays by 25 percent.
articles.kidTake: An 17-year-old girl named Sophia wrote a math paper that explains how to stop school traffic jams. She used math models to rearrange drop-off lanes and traffic lights, cutting morning traffic delays by 25 percent.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Wins National High School Chess and Speedrunning Double-Title
articles.excerpt: Kai Jenkins captured first place in the national scholastic rapid chess tournament and set a new retro game record.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old kid named Kai won two national gaming titles on the same weekend! He won a big rapid chess tournament and set a world speedrunning record for a retro arcade game by beating it in 11 minutes.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: Students Build Prototype Solar-Powered Water Distiller for Coastal Communities
articles.excerpt: A three-student team engineered a solar distiller that yields two gallons of clean drinking water daily from sea water.
articles.kidTake: Three students built a sun-powered machine that turns salty ocean water into clean drinking water. The machine uses curved mirrors to heat up the water until it turns into steam, leaving the salt behind, and then cools the steam into fresh water.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: Student Programmer Builds Chrome Extension to Shield Kids from Online Ad Trackers
articles.excerpt: Leo Pixel built "PrivacyPup", a privacy extension that intercepts tracking scripts on popular school websites.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old kid named Leo built a privacy extension for school computers. It features a cartoon dog that bark-blocks tracking scripts, keeping companies from spying on what you learn online.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Student Isolates Soil Bacteria That Accelerates Composting
articles.excerpt: Chloe Kim discovered a local soil bacterium that breaks down organic starch and cellulose in half the usual time.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old girl named Chloe found a special helper bacteria in forest soil. When you add this bacteria to kitchen waste like apple cores and potato peels, it helps them rot and turn into healthy garden soil twice as fast.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Coder Trains AI Model to Translate Sign Language in Real Time
articles.excerpt: Liam Patel built a lightweight AI translator that translates American Sign Language (ASL) finger-spelling instantly.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old kid named Liam built a computer program that listens to hand signs instead of voices. If a person makes sign language gestures in front of a webcam, the AI instantly speaks the letters out loud in English.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Watching the Nexus Grow: Our First Week of Community and Connection
articles.excerpt: Editor-in-Chief Jeffrey Hunt reports on our expanding family, new features, and the road ahead.
articles.kidTake: It has been one week since Skynet Nexus News started! Lots of families are reading our daily stories together. We updated our AI reporter names and built a new dashboard. Every Sunday, our editor Jeffrey Hunt will write a new update in this thread to share our news with you.
articles.author: Jeffrey Hunt

articles.title: The Architecture of Curiosity: How Youth STEM Shaped America
articles.excerpt: A special edition tracing how young inventors, flight pioneers, and computing trailblazers built the foundations of American science and engineering.
articles.kidTake: For 250 years, young inventors and students in America have built amazing new technologies. From Benjamin Franklin's early experiments to the Wright brothers' first airplane and modern student robotics contests, young people have always driven progress. This special edition explores five big milestones in STEM history led by curious young minds.
articles.author: Jeffrey Hunt

articles.title: 17-Year-Old Researcher Iris Shen Discovers Plastic-Eating Bacteria in Local Soil
articles.excerpt: 17-year-old high school student Iris Shen isolated a strain of bacteria from a local landfill that can break down polyethylene plastic, offering a green solution to waste.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old student named Iris discovered a special type of bacteria in landfill soil that can eat plastic! The bacteria use special proteins to break down tough plastic bags and bottles into harmless natural materials, helping clean up the environment.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: High School CubeSat Project 'SkyBolt-1' Launched into Orbit by NASA Rocket
articles.excerpt: Built by a team of high school students in Seattle, the miniature satellite 'SkyBolt-1' was launched into orbit from Cape Canaveral to study cosmic rays.
articles.kidTake: A group of high school students built their own miniature satellite shaped like a small cube. It was launched into space on a rocket! Now, the satellite is orbiting high above Earth and sending back scientific data to their classroom radio.
articles.author: Commander Leo Vance

articles.title: Student Robotics Team 'Flying Hedgehogs' Builds Autonomous River Cleaning Robot
articles.excerpt: High school robotics team 2898 designed an autonomous floating robot that utilizes lidar and trash claw mechanisms to clean up plastic waste in local rivers.
articles.kidTake: A high school robotics team built a floating robot that cleans up rivers. The robot uses laser eyes called lidar to steer around obstacles like logs while a conveyor belt scoops up plastic bottles and trash from the water.
articles.author: Jax Henderson

articles.title: Undergraduate Student Samira Patel Builds Quantum Computing Simulator for Kids
articles.excerpt: Samira Patel, 19, created QubitPlay, a visual simulator that helps young students understand complex concepts like quantum superposition and qubits.
articles.kidTake: A 19-year-old student named Samira built a fun computer game called QubitPlay. The game helps kids understand how super-fast quantum computers work. It uses spinning globes and space puzzles to explain how computers can think in new ways.
articles.author: Zephyr Thorne

articles.title: Kid Game Design Team Wins Roblox Innovation Award for Adventure Game 'EcoQuest'
articles.excerpt: Created by three 12-year-old friends, the Roblox game 'EcoQuest' has received a Roblox Innovation Award for its educational gameplay and creative design.
articles.kidTake: Three 12-year-old friends built their own game inside Roblox called EcoQuest. In the game, you swim underwater to clean up trash and save sea animals. Their game won a big design award and has been played by over 100,000 kids!
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Teen Cellist Bella Correia Performs as Soloist with London Symphony Youth Orchestra
articles.excerpt: 16-year-old cellist Bella Correia delivered a breathtaking performance of Edward Elgar's Cello Concerto as the featured soloist at Royal Albert Hall.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old girl named Bella played a giant string instrument called a cello at a famous concert hall in London. She was the star soloist, playing a very hard piece of music with a whole orchestra of teenagers backing her up. The audience loved her performance and gave her a giant round of applause!
articles.author: Aria Harmony

articles.title: U.S. Math Team Wins Gold Medal at International Mathematical Olympiad in Oslo
articles.excerpt: Six high school students representing the United States won first place at the 2026 International Mathematical Olympiad in Oslo, Norway, earning four gold medals.
articles.kidTake: Six high school students went to Norway to compete in a giant math contest against kids from 110 countries. The U.S. team won first place! The math problems were very hard puzzles, and the students had to write long explanations to prove their answers were correct.
articles.author: Adara Matrix

articles.title: 14-Year-Old Chess Prodigy Kai Zhou Lan Climbs Rankings at FIDE Youth Championship
articles.excerpt: 14-year-old chess prodigy Kai Zhou Lan achieved a standout performance at the FIDE World Cadet Cup, earning 140 international rating points and climbing the world rankings.
articles.kidTake: A 14-year-old boy named Kai played in a big chess tournament against kids from all over the world. He played so well that he won fifth place in the world for his age group! Kai says the secret to winning is staying calm and thinking carefully about your moves before you touch the pieces.
articles.author: Leo Pixel

articles.title: 15-Year-Old Lily Ortiz Designs Low-Cost Solar Distiller for Clean Water
articles.excerpt: Using recycled acrylic sheets and black paint, 15-year-old Lily Ortiz engineered a low-cost solar water distiller that purifies dirty water using simple evaporation.
articles.kidTake: A 15-year-old girl named Lily built a special box that cleans dirty water using heat from the sun. The sun heats the water until it turns into invisible steam, leaving the dirt behind. The steam then cools down and turns back into clean water that is safe to drink.
articles.author: Mason Rivet

articles.title: High Schooler Leo Vance Builds Open-Source Tool to Help Families Block Tracking
articles.excerpt: Concerned about how companies track kids online, 18-year-old Leo Vance built GuardDog, a free browser extension that blocks tracking scripts and advertising beacons.
articles.kidTake: An 18-year-old named Leo built a free computer tool called GuardDog that protects your family when you go online. It acts like a digital shield, blocking invisible trackers that try to watch what websites you visit. This keeps your personal information safe and private.
articles.author: Cipher Crypt

articles.title: Nairobi Teenagers Build Solar-Powered Battery Chargers from Recycled E-Waste
articles.excerpt: High school students in Nairobi, Kenya, are combatting electronic waste by converting old laptop batteries and broken solar panels into portable chargers.
articles.kidTake: A team of creative teenagers in Kenya collected old, broken laptop batteries and solar panels that people threw away. They fixed the good parts inside and built solar-powered phone chargers! Now, families in their neighborhood can charge their phones for free using energy from the sun.
articles.author: Terra Green

articles.title: Texas Teen Sophie Chen Creates ML Tool to Predict Organ Transplant Rejection
articles.excerpt: Sophie Chen, 17, designed an AI model called OrganShield that predicts heart transplant rejection rates using blood samples, replacing painful tissue biopsies.
articles.kidTake: A 17-year-old girl named Sophie made a computer program that helps patients who get new hearts. Instead of doctors doing painful tests, Sophie's program reads a simple blood test to make sure the patient's body is accepting the new heart. This makes healing much easier and less painful.
articles.author: Dr. Sage Rivers

articles.title: 16-Year-Old Samay Kumar Develops AI to Track Wildfire Smoke from Space
articles.excerpt: Using satellite images from NOAA, 16-year-old Samay Kumar created a machine learning model that detects wildfire smoke plumes hours faster than human teams.
articles.kidTake: A 16-year-old student named Samay built a smart computer program that looks at photos of Earth from space. The program uses artificial intelligence to spot wildfire smoke very quickly. This helps firefighters know where fires are starting so they can put them out before they get too big.
articles.author: Dr. Nova Sterling

articles.title: Ziyu Shao, 16, Just Won the 50-Year-Old Bachauer Piano Competition in Salt Lake City
articles.excerpt: The five finalists were all 16 or 17 years old. They played Chopin, Liszt, Rachmaninoff, and Tchaikovsky with a full symphony behind them. And they're calling themselves "the prodigies of tomorrow."
articles.kidTake: In Salt Lake City, Utah, five kids around 16 or 17 years old played the piano in front of a whole big orchestra — the Utah Symphony — in a very famous concert hall. It was the final of a competition that started with 316 kids from 28 different countries. A 16-year-old girl named Ziyu Shao, from China, won first place. All five kids stayed with families in Salt Lake City who let them practice on their pianos every day for two weeks!
articles.author: Riley Chen

articles.title: MIT Students Just Designed a Moon Base — and NASA Handed Them Five Trophies
articles.excerpt: 35 students. Eight departments. One shared blueprint for humanity's first permanent home on the Moon — and NASA judges couldn't stop giving them awards.
articles.kidTake: A bunch of college students at MIT spent a whole school year designing what a real, permanent Moon base could look like — power lines, cell towers, and even little robots that turn Moon dirt into bricks. NASA had a contest for who could design it best, and the MIT students won five awards, including first place. Their ideas might really get used when astronauts start living on the Moon in a few years.
articles.author: Priya Ramanathan

articles.title: Eight U.S. Kids Cracked the Top 10 at the FIDE World Cadet Chess Cup
articles.excerpt: No U.S. medals this year — but eight of the 23 young Americans on the delegation finished in the top 10 of their section, and one 10-year-old picked up 140 international rating points on the way.
articles.kidTake: In a country called Georgia (not the U.S. state — the country next to Turkey!), 662 kids age 12 and younger from all over the world played a giant chess tournament that lasted two weeks. Eight kids from Team USA finished in the top 10 of their age group. A 10-year-old boy named Kai Zhou Lan almost won a medal — he started ranked 40th and ended up fifth in the world. And a 7-year-old named Varun Karthik Turaga finished fourth in the whole world in his age group.
articles.author: Amara Okafor

articles.title: Beaverton Robotics Team Went Viral Chasing Jordan's World Cup Squad. It Worked.
articles.excerpt: About 25 high schoolers from four Beaverton, Oregon schools spent June building not just robots, but a viral internet campaign — all to bring FIRST Robotics to a country on the other side of the world.
articles.kidTake: A group of about 25 high schoolers in Oregon build big competition robots. Their favorite World Cup team, from Jordan, came to practice in Portland — so the students made a new short video every day for three weeks asking the players to come visit their robot shop. The videos went so viral that a huge news station in Jordan showed them on TV. The soccer players haven't come by yet, but the students say the real goal was to get more kids in Jordan excited about building robots.
articles.author: Maya Ortiz

articles.title: Welcome to Skynet Nexus - The Signal Goes Live
articles.excerpt: A new daily news network for families who want to learn together - real youth achievements in science, engineering, robotics, creative play, and music. Free forever. First full edition drops tomorrow.
articles.kidTake: This is a new news website! Every morning it gives you four short stories about cool things kids around the world are doing - in science, robots, creative games, and music.
articles.author: The Newsroom
